facts.text: In the 1800s locals called the forested area of South Venice “the pinewoods” and tried various short‑lived farms.
The 1920s saw a railroad from Nocatee Freight Company to the sawmill town of Woodmere, linking the area to regional trade.
In 1952 two New Jersey brothers bought 3,000 acres and plotted 19,587 lots, founding the South Venice Subdivision.
In 1956 developers dedicated 1,600 feet of Gulf Beach to residents, adding bridges that later vanished when the Intracoastal Waterway was dredged.
The Corps compensated the loss by buying a ferry and building docks, restoring beach access after the 1965 waterway construction.
South Venice sits west of North Port, south of Venice, and north of Englewood, placing it in the heart of the Sarasota‑Sarasota‑Venice metro area.
